CrickArena is a full-stack cricket management platform featuring a tournament engine, AI lineup optimizer, and real-time analytics — built end-to-end as my final year MCA project.
⚙️ Real-time match analytics
Custom statistical engine delivering live win probability, momentum tracking, pressure index, and phase-aware predictions (powerplay / middle / death). Updates are pushed via WebSockets with sub-50ms latency.
🤖 AI-powered lineup optimizer
Generates Playing XI + 3 substitutes across Balanced / Aggressive / Defensive strategies. Players are evaluated using 5 performance factors through a hybrid ML + rule-based model with explainable reasoning per selection.
🏆 Tournament engine
Automated fixture generation for League, Knockout, and Group+Knockout formats. Standings and scorecards update automatically after every result — zero manual input after setup.
🎫 End-to-end ticket booking
SVG stadium map → seat selection → dynamic pricing → Razorpay → QR ticket → automated email. No third-party booking service — every step custom built.
🤝 Sponsorship management
Marketplace with tiered packages, digital agreements with e-signatures, auto-generated PDFs, and payment audit trails.
🔐 Security-first, production-grade architecture
Firebase Auth + Google OAuth · RBAC middleware · rate limiting · HTTP-only cookies · Joi validation. Designed with production readiness in mind.
👥 Six role-based ecosystems
Built with strict permission boundaries and dedicated workflows to mirror a real-world cricket setup:
• Admin: Oversees the platform, approves clubs, and monitors system health
• Club Manager: Manages club operations, team administration, and tournament registration
• Coach: Uses the AI optimizer for match-day strategies and manages active squads
• Player: Tracks career statistics, match participation, and fitness records
• Sponsor: Explores tiered B2B packages and executes digital contracts
• Fan / User: Engages with live match analytics and books tickets via the interactive stadium interface
Additional capabilities
Real-time in-club messaging · player career dashboards · training management · moderated gallery · personalized role dashboards · global search with autocomplete
Tech Stack
Vue 3 + Vite · Node.js + Express · Python (scikit-learn) · MongoDB Atlas · Firebase · Socket.IO · Razorpay · Tailwind CSS
Deployment
Vercel · Render · MongoDB Atlas · Firebase Auth
System Scope
21 data models · 20+ API modules · 70+ pages · AI engine · analytics pipeline · PDF / QR / SMTP systems · in-app messaging
This is what a final year project looks like when you build it like a real product.

Accenture fired 11,000 people.
Then sent an email to everyone who survived.

The email said: if you are not using AI tools regularly, you will not be promoted. Full stop.
Not "we encourage AI adoption." Not "AI skills will be a bonus."
Use it. Or stay where you are. Forever.

And they're not just saying it. They're tracking it.
Every week. How often you log into their AI platform. How much you're using it. It goes directly into your performance file.

Most people reacted to this news with outrage. "How dare they?"
"This is dystopian."
"Employees deserve better."

Here's my honest take — and it might be unpopular.
Accenture is being more honest than almost any other company right now.
Every major company in India and the world is moving toward AI-first operations.
TCS lost 23,000 people this year. Oracle fired 12,000 in a morning email. The entire industry is restructuring around a simple truth: AI can do what large teams used to do, faster and cheaper.

Most companies are doing this quietly. Shrinking through attrition. Freezing hiring. Not replacing people who leave. Letting the workforce slowly hollow out while saying nothing.

Accenture looked at their employees and said: here is exactly what we need from you. Here is the new standard. Meet it — or we will part ways.
That is harsh. But it is honest.

The employees who are angry about this email are the same employees who, two years from now, will be shocked when they get laid off "due to restructuring." Because nobody told them the bar had moved.
At least Accenture told them.

The uncomfortable truth about AI in 2026 is this:

The skill gap is not a mystery. Every company knows which employees are building AI into their workflow and which ones are waiting for it to blow over. The ones waiting are not safe. They're just uninformed.

AI is not going to pause while you decide if you like it.
The new contract of employment in tech is simple: learn the tools, use the tools, prove the tools make you better — or make way for someone who will.
That's not dystopian. That's the job now.

⚠️ The companies not sending this email are the ones you should worry about. At least Accenture told you the rules before they changed the game.

DAY 8 OF 30 ➔ The Email Labels That Changed How I Manage Communication

Most inboxes look like a filing cabinet that exploded.

No structure. No system. No way to find anything fast.

The fix isn’t a new email app.

It’s 10 labels. Set up in 10 minutes. Used forever.

Here’s the exact label system I build for every client:

THE 10 LABELS THAT RUN EVERY INBOX:

🔴 URGENT :Needs response today. No exceptions.
Rule: Maximum 3 items at any time. If everything is urgent, nothing is.

🟡 THIS WEEK :Important but not on fire.
Rule: Cleared by Friday. No carryover.

🟢 FOLLOW UP : I sent something. Waiting for a reply.
Rule: Auto-flagged for follow-up in 48 hours if no response.

🔵 WAITING ON THEM : Ball is in their court.
Rule: Reviewed every morning. Nothing waits more than 5 days without a chase.

⭐ VIP : Investors, key clients, board members, top leads.
Rule: Responded to within 1 hour. Always.

💼 DEALS :Active pipeline and revenue conversations.
Rule: Reviewed twice daily. No deal goes cold.

📤 DELEGATED : Handled by me. Client doesn’t need to see it.
Rule: Closed when resolved. Client only sees outcomes.

📝 REFERENCE :No action needed. Important for future use.
Rule: Filed immediately. Never sits in inbox.

📅 EVENTS & TRAVEL : Conferences, flights, hotels, logistics.
Rule: Synced to calendar same day. Nothing missed.

🚨 LEGAL & FINANCE : Contracts, invoices, compliance.
Rule: Forwarded to relevant party within 24 hours. Never delayed.

HOW THE SYSTEM WORKS IN PRACTICE:

Every email that arrives gets a label within 10 seconds.
No email sits un labelled.
No email gets lost.

Result:
→ Response time reduced by 60%
→ Zero missed VIP emails
→ Deal pipeline reviewed twice daily , no cold leads
→ Client spends 10 minutes on email instead of 2+ hours

THE RULE THAT MAKES IT ALL WORK:

If it’s in your inbox, it’s waiting to be labelled.
If it’s been labelled, it’s being managed.
If it’s been managed, it’s not your problem anymore.

Set this up today. Your future self will thank you.

DAY 8 OF 30 ➔ The Email Labels That Changed How I Manage Communication

Most inboxes look like a filing cabinet that exploded.

No structure. No system. No way to find anything fast.

The fix isn’t a new email app.

It’s 10 labels. Set up in 10 minutes. Used forever.

Here’s the exact label system I build for every client:

THE 10 LABELS THAT RUN EVERY INBOX:

🔴 URGENT :Needs response today. No exceptions.
Rule: Maximum 3 items at any time. If everything is urgent, nothing is.

🟡 THIS WEEK :Important but not on fire.
Rule: Cleared by Friday. No carryover.

🟢 FOLLOW UP : I sent something. Waiting for a reply.
Rule: Auto-flagged for follow-up in 48 hours if no response.

🔵 WAITING ON THEM : Ball is in their court.
Rule: Reviewed every morning. Nothing waits more than 5 days without a chase.

⭐ VIP : Investors, key clients, board members, top leads.
Rule: Responded to within 1 hour. Always.

💼 DEALS :Active pipeline and revenue conversations.
Rule: Reviewed twice daily. No deal goes cold.

📤 DELEGATED : Handled by me. Client doesn’t need to see it.
Rule: Closed when resolved. Client only sees outcomes.

📝 REFERENCE :No action needed. Important for future use.
Rule: Filed immediately. Never sits in inbox.

📅 EVENTS & TRAVEL : Conferences, flights, hotels, logistics.
Rule: Synced to calendar same day. Nothing missed.

🚨 LEGAL & FINANCE : Contracts, invoices, compliance.
Rule: Forwarded to relevant party within 24 hours. Never delayed.

HOW THE SYSTEM WORKS IN PRACTICE:

Every email that arrives gets a label within 10 seconds.
No email sits un labelled.
No email gets lost.

Result:
→ Response time reduced by 60%
→ Zero missed VIP emails
→ Deal pipeline reviewed twice daily , no cold leads
→ Client spends 10 minutes on email instead of 2+ hours

THE RULE THAT MAKES IT ALL WORK:

If it’s in your inbox, it’s waiting to be labelled.
If it’s been labelled, it’s being managed.
If it’s been managed, it’s not your problem anymore.

Set this up today. Your future self will thank you.

Most experienced mechanical engineers, I know are sitting on powerful problems but often hesitant,
To use AI at work because of one fear: "What if I accidentally share something confidential?"
Here's what took me a while to learn (and now I can't unsee it):

The Golden Rule → Methodology YES. Data NEVER.

Coming from a mechanical engineering background in the energy sector, I used to think AI tools were either too generic or too risky for real plant work.

Then the mindset shift happened.
A few learnings,
You don't give AI your P&IDs. 
You don't paste your client's contract terms. 
You don't share your equipment serial numbers or test results.

But you absolutely CAN ask AI: 
✅ How do I approach a thermal coupling design problem? 
✅ What does ASME BPVC Section VIII say about this? 
✅ Help me structure a failure analysis report 
✅ What's the standard methodology for this validation?

Shape of the problem = shareable. Substance of the problem = stays on site.

Once engineers I've worked with made this switch, productivity didn't just improve  it compounded.

Energy Systems × Product Thinking × AI-enabled Problem Solving

Hiring for consulting / energy / climate roles
Building something in energy + AI
Or want to explore how engineers can use AI without risking data

In 2024, GCCs were racing to hire prompt engineers.
In 2026, that job title barely exists.

It is not that AI got less important.
It is that GCCs misunderstood what AI talent actually means.

Let me explain what happened.
When generative AI exploded onto the scene, the instinct of most organizations was to hire specialists. People who knew how to talk to models. Prompt engineers. GenAI leads. Centers of Excellence staffed with 20 people writing prompts and running experiments.
The hypothesis: isolate the AI talent, let them build, then scale the learning.
The reality: the CoE produced impressive demos. The rest of the organization changed nothing.
The prompt engineering bubble burst not because prompting is unimportant, but because you cannot centralize what needs to be distributed.
AI effectiveness in a GCC is not a specialist skill. It is a literacy.
The finance analyst who redesigns her month-end close process with AI agents is more valuable than the GenAI CoE that built a demo no one deployed. The legal reviewer who builds his own AI-assisted contract review workflow is moving the needle more than the prompt library nobody maintains.
The GCCs getting this right have stopped hiring AI specialists and started building AI fluency across their entire workforce. They have embedded AI into onboarding, into performance metrics, into the daily habits of the org.
The question is no longer: do we have AI talent?
The question is: does every person in our GCC know how to use AI to do their job differently?
If the answer is no, the specialist team you built is a very expensive band-aid.

Is your GCC still treating AI as a specialist function, or have you started building it as an org-wide capability? What actually worked in making that shift?

The Hybrid Team model is replacing full outsourcing, here's why
72% of modern tech companies now use a hybrid model: in-house core team + external specialist team.

Why is full outsourcing fading?

→ It loses institutional context fast
→ Communication overhead kills velocity
→ Hard to scale down without contract drama

Why is pure in-house slowing companies down?

→ Hiring takes 3–8 weeks per role
→ Salary expectations are rising 15% YoY
→ Specialists (AI, security, mobile) are scarce

Hybrid wins because you keep control and gain speed.

Core Team = your domain knowledge
Outsourcing Partner = execution velocity + specialist skills

It's not either/or anymore.

· · · · ·
On March 31, 2026, Oracle sent early-morning termination emails to an estimated 30,000 employees — with no prior warning and immediate system lockouts. It became the largest single-day layoff in tech company history. And it wasn’t alone.
In the first quarter of 2026, over 52,000 tech sector jobs were cut, with approximately 20.4% of layoffs explicitly linked to AI and automation by the companies themselves — a dramatic increase from fewer than 8% in 2025. 
· · · · ·
The scorecard of single-day cuts in 2026:
🔴 Oracle — 30,000 (AI datacenter restructuring)
🟠 Amazon — 16,000 (operational streamlining)
🔵 Dell — 11,000 (cost-cutting / restructuring)
⬛ Block — 4,000 (40% of workforce — AI cited directly)
🔵 Meta — 2,000+ (Reality Labs + division cuts)
🟡 Ericsson — 1,900
🟢 ASML — 1,700 (management & support roles)
🔵 Atlassian — 1,600 (“AI changes the mix of skills we need”)
🎮 Epic Games — 1,000+
🛒 eBay — 800
· · · · ·
📌 What’s different about 2026:
The 2023–2024 layoffs were a correction of pandemic over-hiring. The 2026 wave is structurally different — companies are now explicitly replacing human roles with AI systems, not just trimming headcount. 
Atlassian’s CEO said it directly: “It would be dishonest to pretend AI doesn’t change the mix of skills we need or the number of roles required in certain areas.” That level of candor is rare — but the trend is universal.
· · · · ·
💡 The Procurement & Supply Chain Lens:
Every company on this list is either a major technology supplier, a platform your team sources through, or a partner in your digital procurement stack. When Oracle, Amazon, and Dell restructure at this scale, it affects SLAs, account management quality, contract terms, and innovation roadmaps.
Procurement leaders must now ask: is my supplier base AI-resilient? Are key contacts still employed? Are the platforms we depend on being rebuilt around fewer humans?
The workforce transformation isn’t coming. It’s already here.
· · · · ·
Source: CNBC / Layoffs.fyi / Business Standard / Tech-Insider, 2026

Missouri just ousted half its city council over a $6 billion AI data center.
In Indianapolis, a councilmember who voted woke up to “No Data Centers.” 

$98 billion in data center projects were blocked or delayed in Q2 2025 alone across 11 states. Opposition rose 125% that quarter. Only 26% of Americans hold a favorable view of AI.
Why?
Electricity prices jumped 6.9% in 2025 more than double headline inflation. Data centers now account for 40% of electricity demand growth through decade’s end. Near major clusters, wholesale power costs are up 267% over five years. 21 million U.S. households are behind on utility bills. Prices could rise another 40% by 2030. 78% of Americans are worried data centers will raise their energy bills. 
The political fallout is already here.
Utility costs are the new “cost of eggs.” Sanders and AOC introduced the AI Data Center Moratorium Act in March 2026.

Foundation model economics never priced political permission as a binding constraint. It was assumed free. Missouri is the first time that variable showed up with a non-zero number inside the compute cost curve.
Three things need to change:
1. Community equity architecture. Not charity but contracts. Bind compute revenue back to host communities. Rate protection. Local hiring. Infrastructure investment.

2. Site selection discipline. Brownfields in distressed secondary cities such as Gary, Youngstown, East St. Louis. Existing power infrastructure. Governments that want the investment and jobs

3. Hyperlocal accountability. Property taxes paid. Jobs created. Rate caps guaranteed. Local supplier spend committed.

There are some real alternatives. 

Remote federal land + SMR nuclear. The U.S. has 640 million acres of federal land. Much of it sits on existing transmission corridors. Co-locate small modular reactors, build hyperscale compute away from populated grids, and remove the local political variable entirely. Wyoming, Nevada, and Idaho are already in conversations.

Texas concentration with eyes open. ERCOT, permitting speed, no federal oversight, existing momentum. The near-term path of least resistance. But concentrating 40% of American AI compute on a single grid that nearly collapsed in February 2021 is a sovereign risk

Industrial brownfields. Gary. Youngstown. Flint. East St. Louis. These are cities with existing high-voltage transmission infrastructure, shuttered industrial land, and local governments that will negotiate hard for the investment.

Distributed modular deployments. Smaller footprints co-located with existing industrial and utility infrastructure. No single point of political or grid failure.


Offshore platforms and orbital compute. Serious money is moving here. 
Microsoft, Google, and SpaceX are all running parallel tracks. Orbital compute integrated with low-latency satellite networks is a 10-year horizon

The country that solves the community compact at scale doesn’t just win the infrastructure race. It wins the century.

"A paper last month by LSE professor Luis Garicano and co-authors extends the idea that jobs are bundles of tasks, to consider whether the different activities in a job are a tightly bound bundle or something more akin to an itemised list of discrete activities. In the context of software, a contractor or junior hire generally falls into the latter group: these jobs are weak bundles, with daily work consisting mainly of writing code to spec — tasks that could be given to someone else (or AI) without any disruption to the workflow or the quality of the final product. Here, AI breaks off a large chunk of the job and leaves a role with substantially diminished scope (or obviates the need for that hire or contract). 

But senior developers, or coders working outside the tech industry in roles where their programming skills are combined with domain-specific expertise, tend to have jobs comprising tightly enmeshed and cross-functional tasks. Here extracting the coding part of the job from all the rest is much harder, so the bundle of tasks remains intact. Instead of becoming a competitor AI becomes an assistant, enhancing rather than eroding the job. This fits with the findings from Brynjolfsson and our own analysis that hiring for senior software roles continues to hold up better than for junior ones."

I spent over 10 years working in construction, and a big part of the job was staying on top of documents. Drawings, specs, RFIs, contracts, etc. There was always something to track down, compare, or double-check.

Getting out into the field actually felt like a luxury. At times, it felt like the building part was passing us by.

That’s something I’ve always felt we could do better as an industry.

Today, I’m proud to share that we’ve raised $10M in seed funding for Primepoint, led by Navitas Capital with participation from Penny Jar Capital, NextView Ventures, GS Futures, and AGLAÉ VENTURES.

Thank you to Matthew Strozier at The Wall Street Journal for covering our story.

Construction projects run on drawings, but the information teams need is spread across hundreds of pages and multiple documents. Staying aligned means constantly searching, comparing revisions, and manually connecting information across multiple documents.
  
We’re building Primepoint to bring that information together and streamline the work teams normally have to do by hand. That includes identifying conflicts, drafting RFIs, and reviewing submittals against project documents.

For me, this is about helping teams spend less time chasing information and more time focused on actually building.

I feel incredibly lucky to be building this alongside Lubomir Bourdev, whose work in AI has shaped how machines understand visual data, and Hamid Palo, who brings deep product experience and a clear vision for how this should come together.

Grateful to our investors, customers, and everyone who has supported us along the way.

This is something I’ve wanted to see in this industry for a long time.

P.S We’re also hiring. Feel free to reach out if you want to learn more!

I work in operations at Shopify. I'm not an engineer. I've never written code before this year.

Since 2025 I've been going deep on AI not as a side experiment, but as a real part of how I do my job every day. And I want to start sharing what I'm learning.

Here's why.

There have been many layoffs this year and the narrative seems to be that AI is replacing people. If you're in the workforce right now, especially in a non-technical role, it's hard not to feel like the ground is shifting underneath you.

But that's not what I'm experiencing from the inside.

I plan/manage headcount for my job. I build internal tools, wrangle data, run processes that keep hiring and headcount moving. AI hasn't replaced any of that. What it's done is unlock an option that didn't exist before.

Think about how we've always solved business problems in operations. You identify a gap, write a business case, maybe run an RFP, evaluate vendors, negotiate a contract, implement over months and hope the tool actually solves the problem you started with. Or you write a brief for an engineering team, get in the queue, and wait. Either way, the person closest to the problem is the furthest from the solution.

That's what's changed for me. I'm not waiting for a vendor or an engineering sprint anymore. I can build it myself; not because I learned to code, but because I know my domain well enough to describe exactly what needs to happen and why.

Three things top of mind:

1. Domain experts get amplified, not replaced. The more I understand my own work, the edge cases, the downstream systems, the decisions I'm making without even thinking about them the more useful AI becomes.

2. Curiosity matters more than technical skill. Working with AI forced me to get genuinely curious about my own work in a way I never had before. What do I actually do every Monday? What decisions am I making and in what order? Why does this process exist and not some other one? The more I could articulate the details of my world to something that doesn't understand it at all, the more useful it became. That curiosity to pull apart your own work and explain it like you're onboarding someone from scratch — that's the real skill. And the more context you give, the more your tools have to work with.

3. Non-technical people have an underrated advantage here. We know the business problems. We know what breaks when the data is wrong. We know why a process exists. That turns out to be exactly what AI needs to be useful.

I'm not an expert on any of this. I'm an ops person who's learning. Some weeks it works, some weeks it doesn't. But every week I understand my work a little better, and that compounds.

I'm going to start sharing my journey of what I'm building, what's surprising me, what's not working. If you're in ops, revenue, or any non-technical role wondering how AI fits into your actual job, I think you'll relate.

More soon.
*Written by my agent *

What happens when the world’s most powerful AI company pivots from chatbots to combat? 

In this week’s market news recap, our analysts Rithika Jamisetty, Dhruv Manoj, and Jaival Patel covered OpenAI’s Pentagon deal and its implications for the future of AI, defense spending, and corporate power.

OpenAI secured a $200M Pentagon contract after reversing its stance on military AI use and leveraging high-level government hires.

The company quietly lifted restrictions on defense applications in 2024 and built a “revolving door” pipeline by hiring former officials from key U.S. government institutions.

Consumer backlash, employee protests, and reputational damage followed, while Anthropic gained traction as an ethics-first alternative.

Defense firms like Lockheed Martin and Boeing, policymakers in United States Congress, and consumers concerned about surveillance all face significant implications.

The deal may normalize defense alignment across AI companies, accelerate a “race to the ethical bottom,” and outpace existing legal frameworks governing autonomous warfare.

Despite backlash, OpenAI’s success suggests defense partnerships may become essential for AI firms, while growing scrutiny could drive calls for regulation and global AI arms control.


What Experts Are Saying:
- Edward Snowden, former NSA intelligence contractor and whistleblower: “There is only one reason for appointing an [NSA] Director to your board. This is a willful, calculated betrayal of the rights of every person on Earth.”
- Avner Gvaryahu, DPhil Researcher at the University of Oxford: “Process collapses into a 20-second approval of a machine recommendation. And the companies that built and sold the system sit entirely outside the legal framework, because international humanitarian law was designed for states and their agents, and Palantir is not a signatory to the Geneva conventions.”
- Jonathan Taplin, former USC research director and professor: “With AI, the ability to transcribe [every public conversation], to look through it, correlate it all, you could say: This person is a member of the opposition — and make a map of all 100 million.”

US Staffing Industry in 2026: Smarter Hiring, Human Touch
2026 isn’t about mass hiring anymore—
it’s about meaningful hiring.
The US staffing industry (~$183B) is in a reset phase.
No hyper-growth, no slowdown—just smarter, more intentional hiring.


💡 What’s changed?
👉 Quality > Quantity
Clients want precision:
✔️ Right skills
✔️ Culture fit
✔️ Long-term value
Recruiters today aren’t just filling roles—
they’re advisors, strategists, and problem solvers.


🤖 AI is everywhere… but not everything
AI handles:
Screening
Sourcing
Initial interviews
But here’s the edge:
AI can assess profiles, not people.
Human connection still drives decisions.


⚖️ The 2026 paradox
More candidates.
Fewer “perfect fits.”
Why?
Skills are evolving faster than hiring needs.
Companies want ready-to-deploy talent.
Candidates want real opportunities.
Bridging this gap = the real challenge.


🔄 Work is now hybrid
Contract roles
Gig work
Project-based hiring
Flexibility helps companies stay agile.
But for candidates, it often brings uncertainty.


📈 Where demand is strong
🔥 Healthcare
🔥 Tech & AI
🔥 Logistics
🔥 Professional services
Specialization is the new power.


❤️ Final thought
Despite all the tech, staffing is still human at its core.
You can’t automate:
Trust
Relationships
Potential
2026 is not about hiring faster—
it’s about hiring smarter, deeper, and more human.
Because a job isn’t just a role—
it’s someone’s future.

A history of the Accordion.
 
1822, Christian Friedrich Ludwig Buschmann designed a free-reed instrument in Berlin.
 
The concept?
 
Bring home entertainment to the masses.
 
Replace organs and concertinas with something portable, harmonic, and modern.
 
Voila! the accordion was born.

----
 
𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 & 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
 
From the 1840s it spread across Europe, North America, and Russia. By the early 1900s, Italian factories were producing over a thousand units a month.
 
After World War II, it became a mainstream middle-class instrument a symbol of family and economic success!
 
Then came the hype phase.
 
TV shows, variety programs, accordion schools everywhere.
 
Thousands of players producing the same formulaic repertoire. Too many manufacturers chasing the same demographic. Too much supply. Not enough differentiation. Crazy periods of growth.
 
Followed by periods of contraction.
 
Expand and contract. Expand and contract.
 
Sound familiar?

----

𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗖𝘆𝗰𝗹𝗲
 
Over the last half decade, we've seen an accordion-style response to economic cycles.
 
Businesses have responded by acting like accordions.
 
Expanding. Contracting.
 
Hiring. Firing. Hiring. Firing. Hiring. Firing.
 
This is so stupid.
 
This isn't how you build a sustainable business.
 
So many leaders on social and everywhere else, and their entire mandate is expand and contract, expand and contract.
 
If you're a leader and you've got any level of business expertise, you would have seen these cycles over and over again.
 
You would have [invested in / committed to] strategies to create sustained growth.
 
Or controlled growth. Or even better - played a defensible position - without just shattering the lives of hundreds of thousands of people.
 
AI is transforming every business. And if the response is the same accordion playbook — expand, contract, repeat — then nothing changes.
 
So what does the new norm look like without playing this accordion-style business strategy?

----

𝗗𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗵 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗰𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗱𝗶𝗼𝗻
 
The accordion didn't survive the mainstream.
 
Cultural shifts killed it. Rock and roll arrived.
 
The Beatles arrived.
 
The way people enjoyed music changed.
 
And the instrument that had been a symbol of economic success became a cultural afterthought.
 
If the old mantra was "do more with less" - but you couldn't ...
 
Well, that's the new mantra now.
 
Can you do more with less? Can you grow a sustainable business?
 
Or are you creating more unemployment and less disposable income?
 
. . . .
 
Sustainable businesses don't expand and contract.
 
They build repeatable pipeline and sales processes.
 
What's yours?
​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​
